title: Role of Dietary Antioxidants in the Skin. Diet plays a crucial role in both skin health and overall well-being of a person. Diet contains macronutrients, such as proteins, carbohydrates, and fats, and micronutrients, which include a wide range of vitamins and minerals. While macronutrients are essential components of a healthy body, providing most of the daily caloric intake, micronutrients are needed in much smaller quantities but are indispensable for sustaining life. In the United States, the Department of Agriculture and the Department of Health and Human Services collaborate to provide nutrition guidelines for the American population. These guidelines are updated every five years in accordance with the National Nutrition Monitoring and Related Research Act of 1990, a law that ensures dietary recommendations reflect the latest scientific research. Similarly, the European Society for Clinical Nutrition and Metabolism, a leading multidisciplinary organization, holds annual meetings to swiftly disseminate the latest knowledge on clinical nutrition and metabolism worldwide.

sentences.text: Role of Dietary Antioxidants in the Skin.
Diet plays a crucial role in both skin health and overall well-being of a person.
Diet contains macronutrients, such as proteins, carbohydrates, and fats, and micronutrients, which include a wide range of vitamins and minerals.

While macronutrients are essential components of a healthy body, providing most of the daily caloric intake, micronutrients are needed in much smaller quantities but are indispensable for sustaining life.
In the United States, the Department of Agriculture and the Department of Health and Human Services collaborate to provide nutrition guidelines for the American population.

These guidelines are updated every five years in accordance with the National Nutrition Monitoring and Related Research Act of 1990, a law that ensures dietary recommendations reflect the latest scientific research.

Similarly, the European Society for Clinical Nutrition and Metabolism, a leading multidisciplinary organization, holds annual meetings to swiftly disseminate the latest knowledge on clinical nutrition and metabolism worldwide.
Many micronutrients have been found to have antioxidant properties.
Modern medicine has shown a special interest in studying how dietary interventions can aid in reducing oxidative stress.
A healthy diet is essential to maintain the normal integrity of the skin and to protect it from reactive oxygen species-mediated damage.
Evidence suggests that suboptimal nutrition can adversely affect the skin.
For example, distinct types of dermatitis can result from inadequate intakes of ascorbic acid, tocopherols, riboflavin, niacin, pyridoxine, biotin, zinc, and essential fatty acids.
Ascorbic acid, an antioxidant, is an essential cofactor that reduces oxidative damage in combination with other enzymatic and nonenzymatic defenses.
It aids in the synthesis of collagen, decreases melanin production, and enhances immune function.
Ascorbic acid can decrease melanin production due to its ability to reduce the ortho-quinones generated by tyrosinase, the rate-limiting enzyme in melanogenesis.

Similarly, alpha-tocopherol has been shown to protect the epidermis from oxidative stress by reducing prostaglandins, nitric oxide, and ultraviolet B-induced lipid peroxidation, all of which cause skin inflammation and facilitate sunburn.

These functions make ascorbic acid and alpha-tocopherol beneficial ingredients in various cosmetic products for skin disorders such as atopic dermatitis, psoriasis, cutaneous ulcers, melasma, photoaging, and acne.
Fruits, vegetables, and other plant-based products are useful sources of nutrients that can protect skin health when either consumed as food or used as a medicine or cosmetic.
These dietary sources contain polyphenols, which play a significant role in remediating multiple types of environmental stressors.
These are present in a wide spectrum of produce including grapes, berries, and vegetables.

In recent years, over a thousand types of polyphenols have been identified, which include resveratrol, quercetin, curcumin, epigallocatechin gallate, catechin, cyanidin, procyanidin, caffeic acid, and genistein, among others.

These polyphenols have a range of biological activities and have shown benefits in protecting the skin, maintaining their integrity, and serving as adjuvants in the treatment of various skin diseases.
Polyphenols are potent antioxidants that can reduce oxidative stress through a variety of mechanisms and support the skin's defense and regeneration processes.

Many polyphenols have strong anti-inflammatory and immune-regulating qualities, for example, ferulic acid, resveratrol, caffeic acid, and chlorogenic acid, which can regulate the activation of genes that promote inflammation and cytokine production, thereby affecting the makeup of immune system.
In addition to their anti-inflammatory effects, polyphenols offer photoprotective benefits.
For example, apigenin, found in red wine and chamomile tea, as well as polyphenols from green tea, has demonstrated the ability to protect the skin from the harmful effects of ultraviolet radiation.
Polyphenols also stimulate collagen production, which is crucial for maintaining skin elasticity and structure.
For example, a study on human skin cells exposed to ultraviolet radiation found that quercetin and kaempferol may help combat skin sagging and wrinkles.
Proanthocyanidins are a type of polyphenol responsible for the red, blue, and or purple color of fruits.
These are considered superior antioxidants and anti-inflammatory, immunosuppressive, antiangiogenic, and antiproliferative phytochemicals.
Grapes contain a plethora of biologically beneficial polyphenols and other phytochemicals.
Grape seed proanthocyanidins are consumed nowadays as dietary supplement.
The grape polyphenol resveratrol is a potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory agent, which provides its biological effects through multiple mechanisms.
Some of its mechanisms include deactivating peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor gamma, reducing the activity of cyclooxygenase, and stimulating endothelial nitric oxide synthase.
These actions contribute to its powerful effects in combating oxidative stress and inflammation.
Resveratrol has been associated with various health benefits including antiaging, wound healing, and anticancer properties.
Growing evidence supports the idea that grapes play a significant role in promoting skin health and managing skin conditions.
As such, the next section focuses on the phytochemicals found in grapes and their biological activities that are beneficial for the skin.
